items.q: Why are X-linked recessive conditions more common in males?
items.correct: Males have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive allele is expressed — there's no second X to mask it.
items.misconception: X-linked recessive conditions affect more males because the Y chromosome makes the disease worse.
items.random_wrong: X-linked conditions affect only males because females cannot inherit X-linked alleles.
items.why: Students don't grasp hemizygosity — they think the Y chromosome must be active in the inheritance, instead of absent for that gene.

items.q: What does it mean for an allele to be 'dominant'?
items.correct: It is expressed in the phenotype when at least one copy is present (in a heterozygote).
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common allele in a population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele physically destroys the recessive allele in the genome.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'dominant' (most prevalent, strongest) misleads students into thinking it relates to frequency or power.

items.q: Why are frameshift mutations usually more severe than point substitutions?
items.correct: Insertions/deletions of a non-multiple of 3 shift the reading frame, changing every codon downstream of the mutation.
items.misconception: Frameshifts only change the codon at the site of the mutation, like a substitution.
items.random_wrong: Frameshift mutations are usually silent because cells can correct them.
items.why: Students see 'one base inserted' and think 'one amino acid changed', missing that the entire downstream reading frame shifts.

items.q: Why are X-linked recessive disorders more common in males than females?
items.correct: Males only have one X — no second allele to mask the recessive
items.random_wrong: The Y chromosome carries extra copies
items.why: Correct answer: Males only have one X — no second allele to mask the recessive. “Males have more X chromosomes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which type of mutation causes a frameshift?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Insertion of 1 base. “Silent substitution” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A carrier mother (X^H X^h) and unaffected father (X^H Y) have a son. What is the probability (as a decimal) he has haemophilia?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
